original_abstract:
Chlorophylls are abundant plant pigments and significant dietary antioxidants associated with reduced cancer risk. This study investigated the antioxidant mechanisms of chlorophyll $a$ and $b$ and their magnesium-free derivatives, pheophytin $a$ and $b$, using both cellular and chemical models. We employed the comet assay to observe the protection of human lymphocytes against hydrogen peroxide-induced DNA Damage. Additionally, we evaluated free radical scavenging via the 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assay, measured Fe(II) chelating capacity, and assessed the inhibition of copper-mediated lipid peroxidation in human low-density lipoprotein (LDL) through thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) formation. 

Our results demonstrate that all tested chlorophyll and pheophytin compounds exert significant, dose-dependent antioxidant effects. Pheophytins generally exhibited superior activity compared to chlorophylls in these assays. The findings suggest that these pigments mitigate oxidative stress through two primary pathways: the direct scavenging of reactive oxygen species and the chelation of transition metal ions, such as iron and copper, which otherwise catalyze the formation of harmful radicals. These data bridge the gap between chemical studies and biological relevance, highlighting the role of natural chlorophyll derivatives in protecting against oxidative DNA damage and lipid peroxidation.
humanized_abstract:
Title Chlorophylls constitute a class of ubiquitous plant pigments with proven dietary antioxidant activity associated with lower risk of cancer. Thus, the purpose of the present study was to investigate the antioxidant activity of the main chlorophyll forms in plants, namely chlorophyll a and b and their magnesium-free derivatives, pheophytin a and b. For this purpose, the protective effect of these pigments on hydrogen peroxide-induced DNA damage was assessed in human lymphocytes using the comet assay. Moreover, their ability to scavenging free radicals in the 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assay, to chelate Fe(II) ions as well as to inhibit copper-induced LDL oxidation, as measured by the thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) assay, was investigated.

Recently, we showed that a battery of physiologically-relevant antioxidant assays could be used to compare the capacities of a range of different chlorophylls and pheophytins to provide protection against both oxidative DNA damage and lipid peroxidation. We have now determined the doses of the various chlorophyll and pheophytin derivatives that induce 50% of their maximum capacities to act as antioxidants and have derived complete dose-response curves that have been used to determine the relative efficacies of each compound in each of the antioxidant assays. In every assay, all of the chlorophyll and pheophytin compounds tested had significant dose-dependent capacities to act as antioxidants, and generally, their pheophytin derivatives had greater antioxidant capacity than their corresponding chlorophyll derivatives. Our measurements suggest that the antioxidant action of the chlorophylls and pheophytins tested can involve at least two of the mechanisms by which antioxidants in general protect against oxidative stress, i.e. trapping of free radicals and complexation of transition metals which are involved in free radical generating systems such as those catalysed by iron and copper. The information presented here relates the large body of structural information about chlorophyll derivatives derived from physicochemical investigations in vitro to biological systems.
